questions.prompt: What do the special filenames do in the App Router?

choices.text: Each enables specific behavior automatically: `page.tsx` makes a folder a route; `layout.tsx` wraps children persistently (doesn't re-mount across navigations); `loading.tsx` is wrapped in a Suspense boundary; `error.tsx` is wrapped in an error boundary (must be a Client Component); `not-found.tsx` shows when notFound() is called; `route.ts` defines HTTP API endpoints. The framework wires these up by filename — no router config needed. Layouts and error boundaries compose by directory nesting.

questions.explanation: Each filename enables a specific behavior automatically via convention.

questions.prompt: You want marketing pages and the logged-in app to have completely different chrome (header, layout, fonts). How to structure without affecting URLs?

choices.text: Use route groups: `app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx` and `app/(app)/dashboard/page.tsx` produce URLs `/about` and `/dashboard` respectively. Parens in folder names organize files without affecting URLs. Each route group can have its own layout (or even root layout) — `app/(marketing)/layout.tsx` for marketing chrome and `app/(app)/layout.tsx` for app chrome. Lets you ship two visually-distinct experiences from one codebase, both deployed together, with clean URL structure.

questions.explanation: Route groups (parens) organize without affecting URLs; can have distinct layouts.
